explanation_md: Base class constructors run before the derived class constructor body. Destructors run in reverse: `Derived`'s destructor first, then `Base`'s destructor. This ordering is fundamental to the lifetime contract of inheritance.

prompt_md: A `Manager` inherits from `Employee` using `public` inheritance. `Employee` has a `private` member `double salary_`. Can `Manager`'s member functions access `salary_` directly?

options.a: Yes — derived classes can access all members of the base
options.b: Yes — but only through a special `protected` accessor
options.c: No — `private` members are inaccessible to derived classes; use `protected` instead
options.d: No — unless `Manager` is declared as a `friend` of `Employee`

explanation_md: `private` means accessible only by the class's own member functions and friends — not by derived classes. To allow derived classes to access a data member directly, declare it `protected`. To preserve encapsulation, keep it `private` and expose it through a `protected` getter.

options.a: `Dog::greet` is private so it cannot override `Animal::greet`
options.b: Without `virtual` on `Animal::greet`, the call is dispatched based on the static type `Animal*`, always calling `Animal::greet`
options.c: The `Animal*` pointer is null so no function runs
options.d: `Dog` should inherit with `protected` instead of `public` inheritance

explanation_md: Without `virtual`, C++ resolves function calls at compile time based on the *static type* of the expression. `ptr` is declared as `Animal*`, so `ptr->greet()` always calls `Animal::greet`. Adding `virtual` to `Animal::greet` would enable runtime dispatch to `Dog::greet` through a base pointer.

prompt_md: What is the effect of `protected` inheritance (e.g., `class B : protected A`)compared to `public` inheritance?

options.a: All of A's members become private in B
options.b: A's public and protected members both become protected in B; B's further derived classes can still access them but external code cannot
options.c: A's public members stay public; only protected members change
options.d: Protected inheritance is identical to public inheritance

explanation_md: Protected inheritance sets a ceiling of `protected` on the inherited access: A's `public` members are reduced to `protected` in B, and A's `protected` members remain `protected`. This means external code (code that holds a B object) cannot call A's originally-public functions, but classes that further derive from B can still access them. `public` inheritance would leave A's public members public in B.

`run()` calls `A::hello()` explicitly (prints "A") then `B::hello()` explicitly (prints "B"). Scope qualifiers resolve the ambiguity that would arise from calling just `hello()` on a `C` object — without qualifiers, the compiler cannot choose between `A::hello` and `B::hello`.
